nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, well-oxygenated hill streams and rivers with rocky substrate in the Western Ghats; clear, cool water with moderate to strong current

decorPreferences: smooth rounded boulders and cobblestones replicating hill-stream substrate
strong filtration with powerheads to simulate current
open midwater swimming space in the center
robust plants (Java fern, Bolbitis, Anubias) anchored to rocks or driftwood
dimmer lighting or floating plants to reduce glare

diet.notes: Accepts a wide variety of prepared foods: high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and sinking pellets form a good staple. Supplement regularly with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, and mosquito larvae. Blanched vegetables (zucchini, spinach) and spirulina-based flake provide beneficial plant matter. In the wild the species grazes algae and biofilm from rocks as well as invertebrates. Feed small amounts 2–3 times daily to match the high activity level of this species.

goodWith: other large peaceful cyprinids (e.g., giant danios, larger barbs)
avoidWith: slow-moving or long-finned fish (active swimmers may stress them)
small nano fish that may be outcompeted at feeding
delicate or fragile species sensitive to current
fin-nipping targets with flowing fins such as bettas or fancy guppies

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Dawkinsia denisonii; depicts adult with characteristic red lateral stripe and black-and-yellow caudal marking.

summary: The Red-lined Torpedo Barb is a striking, torpedo-bodied cyprinid endemic to the Western Ghats of Kerala, India. Its vivid scarlet lateral stripe and bold black-and-yellow caudal fin have made it highly sought-after in the hobby, contributing to IUCN Endangered status. Best kept in groups of 5+ in large, well-oxygenated, current-driven aquaria.
careNotes: Dawkinsia denisonii (formerly Puntius denisonii; reclassified by Pethiyagoda et al. in 2012 via Sahyadria, then settled as Dawkinsia) is a fast, active mid-water shoaler that demands substantial horizontal swimming space. A tank of at least 300 L with a 150 cm footprint is recommended for a group of five adults; larger is strongly preferred. Filtration must deliver strong, well-oxygenated flow — powerheads or a sump return directed along the tank length help simulate the hill-stream current these fish experience in the wild. Temperature tolerance is notably cool for a tropical fish (18–26°C), and sustained temperatures above 26°C are stressful; this also means the species does well in homes where tropical heating may be inconsistent during cooler months. Maintain pH 6.5–7.8 and moderate hardness. Water quality must be excellent: the species is sensitive to elevated nitrates and dissolved organic load, so regular large water changes (30–40% weekly) are essential. Keep in groups of at least five to seven to distribute any minor social tension and to elicit the natural shoaling behaviour that shows off the species’ best colours. Solitary individuals become reclusive and pale. D. denisonii is listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List, largely due to overcollection for the ornamental trade; hobbyists should seek tank-bred specimens where available. Aquacultured individuals are now produced in Southeast Asia and adapt more readily to aquarium conditions than wild-caught fish. The species is generally not fin-nippy despite its active nature, and it will coexist peacefully with robust tankmates of similar size. It will jump if startled — a tight-fitting lid is essential.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of D. denisonii is rare and considered difficult. Commercial aquaculture has been achieved using hormone-induced spawning protocols, primarily in Southeast Asian fish farms. In home aquaria, successful spawning has been reported only occasionally; the species is egg-scattering and shows no parental care, with eggs prone to fungus. A species tank with soft, slightly acidic water, live foods, and a gradual reduction in temperature to the lower end of the range (around 18–20°C) to simulate seasonal conditions may encourage conditioning. Separating adults after spawning is advisable as the eggs and fry will be eaten. Fry are very small and challenging to raise. Most specimens in the hobby are aquacultured commercially.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dawkinsia denisonii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided scientific name, taxonomy, native range (Western Ghats, Kerala/Karnataka), water parameters (temperature 15–22°C per wild data, recommended aquarium 18–26°C), pH, hardness, adult size (to 15 cm), habitat description, diet, breeding notes, and compatibility guidance. Reclassification history (Puntius → Sahyadria → Dawkinsia, Pethiyagoda et al. 2012) documented.

additional.name: FishBase — Dawkinsia denisonii
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max TL ~15 cm), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Western Ghats river systems of Kerala and Karnataka), temperature range, and IUCN Endangered listing. Confirmed fast-water hill stream habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Denison Barb Care Guide
additional.notes: Provided trade-focused care guidance: group size (5+), tank size (55–125 gal), temperature 60–75°F (15.5–24°C), pH 6.5–7.8, diet (omnivore — flake, frozen foods, vegetables), jump risk (tight lid required), and note on aquacultured availability improving. Confirmed intermediate care level and shoaling requirement.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Denison’s Barb (Dawkinsia denisonii)
additional.notes: Provided background on IUCN Endangered status, collection pressure history, aquaculture developments, and husbandry detail including water flow requirements and tank footprint recommendations.

additional.name: IUCN Red List — Dawkinsia denisonii
additional.notes: Confirmed IUCN Endangered (EN) status, endemic range restricted to the Western Ghats (Kerala and parts of Karnataka), and primary threat as overcollection for the ornamental aquarium trade. Also notes habitat degradation as a contributing factor.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care; captive spawning is rarely achieved without hormone induction. Commercial aquaculture via hormone protocols is the main trade source. Home breeding attempts require cool conditioning temperatures (~18–20°C), soft slightly acidic water, and live-food conditioning. Eggs fungus readily; adult removal after spawning is critical.
